h1: Texas Nonconforming Use Letter for Zoning Disputes and Land Use Protection
metaTitle: Texas Nonconforming Use Demand Letter | Zoning Disputes
metaDescription: Generate a Texas nonconforming use letter to protect your grandfathered property rights. State-specific zoning dispute and land use objection tool.
statuteCitation: Tex. Loc. Gov't Code §§ 211.001-211.018; Tex. Loc. Gov't Code § 245.002
deadline: 10 days to file appeal with the Board of Adjustment after the zoning decision
penalty: Injunctive relief, declaratory judgment, and recovery of vested rights under Chapter 245
introParagraph: If your Texas property has a use that predates a zoning ordinance change, you likely have a legally protected nonconforming use, often called a grandfathered right. Texas law strongly protects these rights through Chapter 211 of the Local Government Code and the vested rights statute in Chapter 245. When a city tries to force you to stop a long-standing use, deny a permit, or claim your property must comply with new rules, a properly drafted nonconforming use letter can stop enforcement before it escalates. Texas cities frequently misapply amortization schedules or wrongly classify lawful prior uses. A timely, well-cited letter puts the municipality on notice, preserves your appeal rights, and creates a record for any later challenge in district court or before the Board of Adjustment.
lawExplanation: Texas zoning authority comes from Chapter 211 of the Local Government Code, which lets municipalities regulate land use but does not allow them to extinguish lawful uses that existed before a zoning change. A nonconforming use is a structure, business, or activity that complied with the law when it began but no longer fits current zoning. Under longstanding Texas case law, including City of University Park v. Benners and Board of Adjustment of San Antonio v. Wende, cities may regulate but cannot arbitrarily terminate nonconforming uses without due process or, in some cases, fair amortization. Texas also provides one of the strongest vested rights statutes in the country under Chapter 245 of the Local Government Code. Once a property owner files an original application for a permit, plat, or development approval, the rules in effect at that time generally govern the project, even if the city later changes its ordinances. This protects landowners from shifting regulations mid-project. Section 211.009 gives the Board of Adjustment power to hear appeals from administrative zoning decisions, grant variances, and interpret ordinances. Appeals must be filed within a reasonable time set by ordinance, commonly 10 to 20 days. If the Board rules against you, Section 211.011 allows judicial review by petition for writ of certiorari to district court within 10 days of the decision. Discontinuance, abandonment, and expansion rules vary widely by city. Houston, notably, has no traditional zoning, while Dallas, Austin, San Antonio, and Fort Worth each maintain detailed nonconforming use chapters in their codes. Knowing which framework applies is essential before sending any demand letter.
letterStrategy: A Texas nonconforming use letter works by formally documenting your grandfathered status and citing the exact statutory and case law authority that protects it. The letter should identify the property, describe when the use began, attach evidence such as old permits, utility records, tax filings, photographs, or sworn affidavits, and explicitly invoke Chapter 211 and, if applicable, Chapter 245 vested rights. Address the letter to the city's zoning administrator, planning director, or city attorney, and copy the code enforcement officer who issued any violation notice. Demand specific relief: withdrawal of the violation, written confirmation of nonconforming status, or issuance of the requested permit. Set a clear deadline, typically 14 to 30 days, for a written response. Texas cities take well-drafted letters seriously because losing a vested rights or nonconforming use case can expose them to attorney fees under the Uniform Declaratory Judgments Act and reversal in district court. The letter also preserves your appeal timeline. If the city does not respond or denies your claim, you have a documented record to take to the Board of Adjustment and, if necessary, to file a writ of certiorari in district court. A strong letter often resolves the dispute without litigation by giving city staff legal cover to back down. Avoid threats; instead, present the law clearly, cite the controlling statutes, and make the cost of denying your claim outweigh the cost of granting it.
proceduralNotes: Board of Adjustment appeals in Texas must be filed within the time set by local ordinance, often 10 to 20 days after the administrative decision. Filing fees range from $100 to $500 depending on the city. Judicial review of a Board decision requires a verified petition for writ of certiorari to district court within 10 days under Section 211.011. Texas justice courts handle small claims up to $20,000, but zoning disputes generally must be filed in district court because they seek injunctive or declaratory relief. The Uniform Declaratory Judgments Act, Civil Practice and Remedies Code Chapter 37, allows recovery of attorney fees. Statute of limitations for related takings or vested rights claims varies. Always confirm deadlines with the specific municipality, as ordinances differ.

faq.q: What qualifies as a nonconforming use in Texas?
faq.a: A nonconforming use is any structure, business, or activity that legally existed before a zoning ordinance changed and now does not comply with current rules. To qualify, the use must have been lawful when established, continuously maintained, and not abandoned. Evidence often includes old permits, business licenses, utility bills, tax records, and photographs. Texas courts protect these uses under Chapter 211 of the Local Government Code, though cities may regulate expansion, intensification, and abandonment under their local ordinances.

faq.q: Can a Texas city force me to stop a nonconforming use?
faq.a: Generally no, but cities can adopt amortization schedules requiring termination after a reasonable period that allows recovery of investment. Texas courts have upheld amortization in cases like City of University Park v. Benners when the schedule is fair. Cities can also terminate nonconforming uses if you abandon them, typically through six months or more of discontinuance, or if you substantially expand or change the use. A nonconforming use letter helps prevent wrongful termination.

faq.q: What is Chapter 245 vested rights and how does it help?
faq.a: Chapter 245 of the Texas Local Government Code freezes the regulations that apply to your project at the moment you file your first permit, plat, or development application. Even if the city later changes zoning, density, or use rules, your project is governed by the original rules. This is one of the strongest vested rights statutes in the United States and frequently overlaps with nonconforming use claims when a city tries to apply new ordinances retroactively to existing projects.

faq.q: How long do I have to appeal a zoning decision in Texas?
faq.a: Appeals to the Board of Adjustment must be filed within the time set by city ordinance, commonly 10 to 20 days after the administrative decision. After the Board rules, you have only 10 days under Section 211.011 of the Local Government Code to file a verified petition for writ of certiorari in district court. These deadlines are strict and jurisdictional. Missing them can permanently waive your rights, so send a nonconforming use letter and file appeals promptly.

faq.q: Do I need a lawyer to send a nonconforming use letter?
faq.a: No, property owners can send these letters themselves, and a well-drafted letter often resolves disputes without litigation. However, if the city denies your claim or files an enforcement action, consulting a Texas land use attorney becomes important, especially before filing in district court. Attorney fees may be recoverable under the Uniform Declaratory Judgments Act if you prevail. Many disputes settle once the city receives a clear, statute-based letter showing you understand your rights under Chapters 211 and 245.
